title: 7 Herbal Remedies for Premenstrual Syndrome in the Form of Creams

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Flavonoids are good for premenstrual syndrome because they help reduce anxiety and inflammation, and also have a balancing effect on hormones, which in turn can ease symptoms such as mood swings, cramps, and breast tenderness.

Iridoid glycosides are good for premenstrual syndrome because they help to calm the nervous system, reduce anxiety and stress, and regulate hormones, which can ease symptoms such as mood swings and breast tenderness.

Agnuside is good for premenstrual syndrome because it helps to reduce stress and anxiety by acting on the brain's neurotransmitters, specifically serotonin and GABA, to promote relaxation and alleviate symptoms such as mood swings and irritability.

Saponins are good for premenstrual syndrome because they help to reduce symptoms of PMS by acting as a natural anti-inflammatory and antioxidant, which can help to calm and soothe the body and mind, reducing anxiety, mood swings, and other symptoms associated with PMS.

remedies.remedy_desc: Vitex agnus-castus cream is a natural remedy that may help ease premenstrual syndrome (PMS) symptoms. This plant-based cream is made from the berries of the chaste tree, which has been used for centuries to balance hormone levels in the body. For women with PMS, it may help reduce symptoms like mood swings, breast tenderness, and bloating. The cream may also help regulate menstrual cycles and reduce cramps. Some women find that it helps them feel more calm and focused, reducing anxiety and stress associated with PMS. By soothing and calming the body, Vitex agnus-castus cream may provide relief from PMS symptoms, making it a popular natural remedy for women seeking alternative solutions. Research suggests that the cream's active compounds may interact with the body's hormone system, reducing prostaglandins, which are hormone-like substances that cause pain and inflammation during menstruation.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Cimicifuga racemosa herbal creams that help with premenstrual syndrome, start by preparing a strong tea from dried Cimicifuga racemosa roots. This can be done by steeping 1-2 teaspoons of the dried roots in a cup of boiling water for 5-7 minutes, then straining the mixture. Next, combine the cooled tea with a carrier oil, such as coconut or olive oil, in a 1:4 ratio. For example, use 1 part Cimicifuga racemosa tea to 4 parts carrier oil. Then, add a thickening agent, such as beeswax or candelilla wax, to the mixture to give it a creamy texture. Melt the wax in a double boiler, then slowly pour it into the tea-oil mixture while whisking constantly. Continue whisking until the mixture thickens and forms a smooth, creamy consistency. Finally, pour the cream into a clean container and store it in the refrigerator to use as needed. This cream can be applied topically to help alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome, such as cramps and mood swings.
remedies.image_alt: Vitex agnus-castus herbal creams for premenstrual syndrome

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Linalool is good for premenstrual syndrome because it helps to reduce anxiety, stress, and mood swings by acting as a natural sedative and anti-inflammatory, promoting relaxation and calming the mind and body.
Linalyl acetate is good for premenstrual syndrome because it has anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties that help reduce anxiety, cramps, and mood swings associated with PMS.
Rosmarinic acid is good for premenstrual syndrome because it has anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce symptoms of anxiety, depression, and mood swings associated with PMS.

remedies.remedy_desc: Lavandula angustifolia, also known as lavender, has been used for centuries to help ease symptoms of premenstrual syndrome (PMS). The cream made from its oil is rich in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds that can help soothe and calm the body. It can help reduce anxiety and stress, which are common symptoms of PMS. The lavender oil in the cream can also help relax the muscles and promote a good night's sleep, which is often disturbed during this time. Additionally, the cream may help ease bloating and cramps, making it a natural remedy for some of the physical symptoms of PMS. Some studies suggest that inhaling the scent of lavender can also help reduce symptoms of PMS, making the cream a great option for those who prefer a more natural approach.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Cimicifuga racemosa herbal creams that help with premenstrual syndrome (PMS), start by harvesting or purchasing dried roots of the Black Cohosh plant. Next, dry the roots in a low-temperature oven or a dehydrator to prevent any damage to the active compounds. Then, grind the dried roots into a fine powder using a spice grinder or coffee grinder. Combine the powder with a carrier oil, such as coconut oil or jojoba oil, in a ratio of about 10% Black Cohosh to 90% oil. Heat the mixture in a double boiler or a heat-resistant bowl set over a pot of simmering water, stirring occasionally, until the mixture reaches a temperature of around 160°F to 180°F (71°C to 82°C). Remove the mixture from the heat and let it cool, then strain it through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve to separate the oil from any remaining plant particles. Transfer the oil to a clean container and store it in a cool, dark place. To make a cream, mix the Black Cohosh oil with a moisturizing base, such as shea butter or beeswax, in a ratio of about 10% oil to 90% base. Heat the mixture until it's smooth and melted, then pour it into a tin or jar and let it cool before use.
remedies.image_alt: Lavandula angustifolia herbal creams for premenstrual syndrome

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Rosmarinic acid is good for premenstrual syndrome because it has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that help reduce stress and anxiety, and also act as a natural anti-depressant to alleviate symptoms such as mood swings and irritability.

Luteolin is good for premenstrual syndrome because it has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that help to reduce stress and anxiety, and also regulate hormonal imbalances that contribute to PMS symptoms.

Apigenin is good for premenstrual syndrome because it has anti-anxiety and anti-inflammatory properties that help to reduce symptoms such as mood swings, breast tenderness, and bloating, promoting relaxation and overall well-being.

remedies.remedy_desc: Melissa officinalis cream is a natural remedy that can help with premenstrual syndrome (PMS) symptoms. It contains a compound called linalool, which has a calming effect on the body. When you're feeling stressed or anxious before your period, linalool can help slow down your heart rate and lower your blood pressure. This can also help reduce breast tenderness and mood swings. Additionally, Melissa officinalis cream has anti-inflammatory properties, which can ease cramps and bloating that often occur during PMS. Furthermore, its antiseptic properties can help soothe any irritations or rashes that may appear on the skin due to hormonal changes. By using Melissa officinalis cream, you can find relief from PMS symptoms and feel more comfortable during this time of the month.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Cimicifuga racemosa herbal creams that help with premenstrual syndrome, start by gathering the necessary ingredients and equipment. You will need dried Cimicifuga racemosa root, a carrier oil such as coconut or olive oil, beeswax, and a double boiler or a heat-proof bowl set over a pot of simmering water. Begin by infusing the dried root in the carrier oil for several hours or overnight, allowing the root's active compounds to dissolve into the oil. Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into a clean bowl, discarding the solids. Next, melt the beeswax in the double boiler or heat-proof bowl, then gradually add the infused oil mixture, stirring constantly to combine. Continue heating and stirring until the mixture reaches a smooth, creamy consistency. Remove from heat and pour the mixture into small containers or tubes, allowing it to cool and solidify before use. Once set, the Cimicifuga racemosa herbal cream can be applied topically to the skin, where it may help to alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome, such as cramps, mood swings, and breast tenderness.
remedies.image_alt: Melissa officinalis herbal creams for premenstrual syndrome

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Gingerol is good for premenstrual syndrome because it helps to reduce inflammation, relax muscles, and balance hormone levels, which in turn can ease symptoms such as mood swings, bloating, and breast tenderness associated with PMS.

Shogaol is good for premenstrual syndrome because it helps reduce inflammation and has anti-anxiety properties, which can ease symptoms such as mood swings, bloating, and breast tenderness associated with PMS.

Gingerols are good for premenstrual syndrome because they help to reduce the severity of symptoms such as mood swings, bloating and breast tenderness by acting on the body's hormone system to balance estrogen levels.
Shogaols are good for premenstrual syndrome because they act as a natural anti-inflammatory and antioxidant that helps to ease menstrual cramps, mood swings, and other symptoms associated with PMS.

Zingiberene is good for premenstrual syndrome because it helps to reduce anxiety and stress by acting as a natural relaxant and reducing inflammation in the body, which in turn helps to ease symptoms such as mood swings, bloating, and breast tenderness associated with PMS.

remedies.remedy_desc: Zingiber officinale, also known as ginger, is a popular remedy for premenstrual syndrome (PMS). The active compounds in ginger, such as gingerols and shogaols, have anti-inflammatory properties that help to ease cramps and discomfort associated with PMS. Ginger also contains a natural chemical called zingerone, which helps to reduce nausea and vomiting that some women experience during their menstrual cycle. Additionally, ginger has been shown to improve mood and reduce stress levels, which can be beneficial for women who experience anxiety and depression during PMS. When applied topically in cream form, ginger's anti-inflammatory properties can help to soothe and calm the skin, reducing the appearance of acne and other skin irritations that may be triggered by hormonal changes during PMS. Furthermore, the moisturizing properties of ginger cream can help to hydrate and nourish the skin, leaving it feeling soft and supple.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Cimicifuga racemosa herbal creams for premenstrual syndrome relief, you will need dried Cimicifuga racemosa root, a carrier oil like coconut or olive oil, beeswax, and a double boiler or a microwave-safe bowl. First, measure out one tablespoon of dried Cimicifuga racemosa root and steep it in two tablespoons of the carrier oil in a small saucepan over low heat for 30 minutes to an hour. This process is called infusion and it helps to extract the plant's active compounds into the oil. Once the infusion is complete, strain the oil through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into a separate container, discarding the solids. Next, melt two tablespoons of beeswax in the double boiler or microwave-safe bowl and then slowly pour in the infused oil while constantly stirring until the mixture is smooth and well combined. Pour the mixture into a small container and let it cool and solidify. Once set, the Cimicifuga racemosa herbal cream is ready for use. Apply a small amount to the affected area 2-3 times a day to help alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome.
remedies.image_alt: Zingiber officinale herbal creams for premenstrual syndrome

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Coumarins are good for premenstrual syndrome because they have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that help reduce stress and anxiety, and also ease symptoms such as mood swings and breast tenderness.

Lignans are good for premenstrual syndrome because they have anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce symptoms such as anxiety, depression, and bloating by regulating hormonal balance and stabilizing mood.

Phenolic acids, also known as Black cohosh, are good for premenstrual syndrome because they have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that help to reduce stress and anxiety, improve mood, and alleviate symptoms such as breast tenderness and water retention.

Tannins are good for premenstrual syndrome because they help to reduce symptoms such as bloating, mood swings, and anxiety by acting as a natural anti-inflammatory and antioxidant, which can help to balance hormone levels and calm the nervous system.

remedies.remedy_desc: Angelica archangelica cream is a natural remedy that can help with premenstrual syndrome (PMS). This cream is made from the root of the angelica plant, which has been used for centuries to ease various health issues. When applied to the skin, it can help reduce inflammation and soothe cramps caused by PMS. The cream contains compounds called sesquiterpenes, which have anti-inflammatory properties that can ease pain and discomfort in the abdomen and lower back. Angelica archangelica cream can also help regulate hormones, which can lead to a reduction in mood swings and irritability often associated with PMS. Additionally, its anti-spasmodic properties can help relax the uterine muscles, reducing cramping and discomfort. By applying the cream to the affected areas, women can experience relief from PMS symptoms, improving their overall well-being during this time of the month.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Cimicifuga racemosa herbal cream that helps with premenstrual syndrome, start by drying and preparing the roots of the Black cohosh plant. You can purchase these roots from a herbal supplier or grow them in your garden. Once dried, grind the roots into a fine powder using a spice grinder or mortar and pestle. Combine this powder with a carrier oil such as coconut or olive oil in a saucepan, and heat it gently on low heat for about 30 minutes to allow the active ingredients to infuse into the oil. Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or coffee filter into a clean bowl, and discard the solids. Next, melt a small amount of beeswax in a separate pan and add it to the infused oil mixture. Stir well to combine, then pour the mixture into a clean glass jar. Allow the cream to cool and set before use. Once set, apply the cream to the affected areas as needed to help alleviate premenstrual symptoms.
remedies.image_alt: Angelica archangelica herbal creams for premenstrual syndrome

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Quercetin is good for premenstrual syndrome because it helps reduce inflammation and relax muscles, which can ease symptoms like bloating, mood swings, and cramps that often occur before menstruation.

Hyperforin is good for premenstrual syndrome because it has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that help to reduce symptoms such as anxiety, depression, and mood swings by regulating the levels of neurotransmitters like serotonin in the brain.
Kaempferol is good for premenstrual syndrome because it helps to reduce anxiety and inflammation in the body, which can cause symptoms of PMS such as mood swings, bloating, and breast tenderness.

Tannins are good for premenstrual syndrome because they help reduce symptoms such as mood swings and bloating by acting as a natural anti-inflammatory and antioxidant, thereby promoting relaxation and reducing stress levels.
Naringenin is good for premenstrual syndrome because it has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that help to reduce stress and anxiety, ease symptoms of PMS, and promote relaxation.

remedies.remedy_desc: Hypericum perforatum cream is a natural remedy that can help with premenstrual syndrome (PMS). The cream is made from the St. John's Wort plant, which has been used for centuries to treat mild depression and anxiety. For women with PMS, the cream's active ingredients, like hyperforin and hypericin, can help reduce symptoms such as mood swings, irritability, and anxiety. These ingredients have anti-inflammatory properties, which can also help ease physical symptoms like bloating and breast tenderness. By reducing inflammation and promoting relaxation, the cream may help women feel more calm and centered during the premenstrual phase. Additionally, some studies suggest that the cream's active ingredients may also help regulate hormonal imbalances that contribute to PMS symptoms.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Cimicifuga racemosa herbal creams that help with premenstrual syndrome, you will need dried roots of the Black Cohosh plant, a carrier oil like coconut or olive oil, beeswax, and a few other simple ingredients. First, steep the dried roots in a carrier oil in a double boiler or a heat-proof bowl set over a pot of simmering water, allowing the mixture to infuse for several hours or overnight. This process helps to extract the beneficial compounds from the Black Cohosh roots into the oil. Next, strain the oil and discard the solids. Then, melt the beeswax in a double boiler or in the microwave, and slowly add the infused oil to the melted wax, stirring constantly. Continue to heat the mixture until the beeswax is fully incorporated, then remove it from the heat and let it cool until it thickens into a creamy consistency. Once set, transfer the cream to a container and store it in the fridge to preserve its potency. Apply a small amount to the affected area as needed to help alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome, such as cramps, mood swings, and bloating.
remedies.image_alt: Hypericum perforatum herbal creams for premenstrual syndrome

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Phenolic acids are good for premenstrual syndrome because they have anti-inflammatory properties that help to reduce symptoms of anxiety, depression, and mood swings associated with PMS.

Isoferulic acid is good for premenstrual syndrome because it has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that help reduce symptoms of anxiety, insomnia, and mood swings associated with PMS, making it a potential natural remedy to alleviate these conditions.

Lignans are good for premenstrual syndrome because they may help reduce symptoms such as mood swings, bloating, and breast tenderness by acting as a natural anti-inflammatory and estrogen balancer in the body.
Actein is good for premenstrual syndrome because it helps to reduce symptoms of anxiety and depression by acting on the brain's neurotransmitters, thereby promoting relaxation and a sense of calm.

Coumarins are good for premenstrual syndrome because they have anti-inflammatory and hormone-regulating properties that help reduce symptoms such as mood swings, bloating, and cramps associated with PMS.

remedies.remedy_desc: Cimicifuga racemosa, also known as black cohosh, is a plant that has been used for a long time to help with symptoms of premenstrual syndrome (PMS). The cream made from this plant contains compounds that can help to relax the muscles in the uterus, which can become crampy and painful during PMS. This can help to reduce the severity of cramps and make them more manageable. Cimicifuga racemosa cream may also help to reduce other symptoms of PMS, such as mood swings and breast tenderness. It works by affecting the body's hormonal balance, which can become disrupted during PMS, leading to these symptoms. Some research suggests that Cimicifuga racemosa cream can be as effective as prescription medicines in reducing PMS symptoms, but with fewer side effects. This makes it a popular choice for women looking for a natural way to manage PMS.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Cimicifuga racemosa herbal cream, you'll need dried root of Cimicifuga racemosa, a carrier oil like coconut or olive oil, beeswax, and a double boiler. First, you'll need to steep the dried root in the carrier oil in a saucepan over low heat for about 4-6 hours, or overnight. This process is called infusion and it allows the active compounds from the plant to dissolve into the oil. Once the infusion is complete, strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into a clean container to separate the oil from the plant material. Next, in a double boiler, melt the beeswax and add it to the infused oil, stirring until the mixture is well combined. The beeswax will help to thicken the cream and give it a smooth texture. Finally, transfer the mixture to a clean jar and let it cool and solidify before use. You can apply the Cimicifuga racemosa cream to the skin as needed to help alleviate premenstrual syndrome symptoms. However, it's essential to note that herbal remedies should be used under the guidance of a healthcare professional, and Cimicifuga racemosa should be used in moderation and in accordance with recommended dosages.
remedies.image_alt: Cimicifuga racemosa herbal creams for premenstrual syndrome

intro_desc: Premenstrual syndrome (PMS) affects many women, causing physical and emotional symptoms like cramps, mood swings, and fatigue. Herbal creams can be a helpful solution. Vitex agnus-castus, also known as chasteberry, is a herb that can help regulate hormones and reduce symptoms of PMS. It has been shown to decrease cramps, bloating, and mood swings. Lavandula angustifolia, or lavender, is another herb that can be beneficial. It has a calming effect on the body, reducing anxiety and stress. When applied topically as a cream, these herbs can be absorbed through the skin, providing relief from PMS symptoms. Fixing PMS issues is important because it can improve daily life. PMS can cause women to miss work, have trouble sleeping, and struggle with relationships. By reducing symptoms, women can feel more in control and confident. Improving PMS symptoms can also lead to better physical health, as some women may experience increased risk of heart disease and depression due to untreated PMS. Using herbal creams with Vitex agnus-castus and Lavandula angustifolia can be a natural and effective way to alleviate PMS symptoms and improve overall well-being.
supplementary_best: A combination of Vitex agnus-castus, Lavandula angustifolia, and Melissa officinalis herbal creams may help relieve premenstrual syndrome (PMS) symptoms. Vitex agnus-castus is known to balance hormone levels, particularly estrogen and progesterone, which can become imbalanced during the menstrual cycle, leading to symptoms like mood swings and bloating. Lavandula angustifolia, or lavender oil, has a calming effect on the mind and body, reducing anxiety and stress that often accompanies PMS. Melissa officinalis, also known as lemon balm, has a mild sedative effect, further helping to calm the nervous system and promote relaxation. This combination of herbs addresses the hormonal, emotional, and physical aspects of PMS, providing a comprehensive approach to symptom relief. The anti-inflammatory properties of Vitex agnus-castus and Lavandula angustifolia may also help alleviate physical symptoms like cramps and breast tenderness.  Note: Before using any herbal remedies, it is essential to consult with a healthcare professional, especially if you have any underlying medical conditions or are taking medications.
supplementary_avoid: If you have premenstrual syndrome (PMS), it's best to avoid creams containing certain herbs that may worsen symptoms. One such herb is 'Ginkgo biloba', which can increase blood flow and cause more menstrual cramps. Another herb to avoid is 'Ginseng', which can stimulate the body and make PMS symptoms worse. 'Saw Palmetto' may also be a problem as it can cause hormonal imbalances, leading to increased PMS symptoms. 'Dong Quai' has been linked to increased bleeding and clotting issues, which can be a concern for people with PMS. 'Red Clover' is high in isoflavones, which can mimic estrogen in the body, potentially worsening PMS symptoms. Finally, 'Wild Yam' can cause uterine contractions and worsen cramps. These herbs may interact with your body in ways that make PMS symptoms worse, so it's best to avoid them.
supplementary_other_remedies: Aromatherapy with essential oils like lavender and peppermint can help ease premenstrual syndrome symptoms. These oils can be inhaled through steam inhalation, added to bath water, or applied to the skin through massage. They help reduce stress and anxiety by affecting the brain's emotional centers.  A warm bath or a hot compress on the lower abdomen can also provide relief from cramps and discomfort. The heat increases blood flow to the area, relaxing the uterine muscles and reducing spasms.  Some people find relief from PMS symptoms by practicing yoga and meditation. These activities help manage stress and anxiety by teaching the body to relax and focus. Deep breathing exercises and visualization techniques can calm the mind and ease symptoms like mood swings and headaches.  Dietary changes, such as increasing the intake of complex carbohydrates like whole grains and fruits, can help stabilize blood sugar levels and reduce symptoms like bloating and mood swings. Drinking plenty of water and limiting caffeine and sugar intake can also help alleviate symptoms.  Acupuncture, a form of traditional Chinese medicine, involves inserting thin needles into specific points on the body to stimulate healing and relaxation. Some people find it helps reduce PMS symptoms by improving hormone balance and reducing stress.
supplementary_related_ailments: People with premenstrual syndrome (PMS) often experience other health issues. One common problem is anxiety and depression. Medicinal herbs like St. John's Wort and passionflower can help. They contain compounds that affect neurotransmitters in the brain, which can ease symptoms of anxiety and depression.  Some people with PMS also have digestive issues, such as bloating and cramps. Ginger and peppermint can help with this. Ginger has anti-inflammatory properties, which can reduce pain and swelling. Peppermint can ease cramps by relaxing the muscles.  Many women with PMS also have fatigue and insomnia. Ashwagandha and valerian root can help with this. Ashwagandha is an adaptogen, which means it helps the body adapt to stress. Valerian root is a natural sedative, which can promote better sleep.  Some people with PMS may experience headaches and migraines. Feverfew and butterbur can help. Feverfew has compounds that can reduce inflammation and pain. Butterbur has anti-inflammatory properties and can ease migraine symptoms.  People with PMS may also have skin issues, such as acne and rashes. Dandelion root and red clover can help. Dandelion root can cleanse the body and reduce inflammation. Red clover contains isoflavones, which can reduce inflammation and promote healthy skin.  Chasteberry and red clover can help with hormonal imbalances, which are common in people with PMS. Chasteberry can regulate estrogen levels and reduce symptoms of PMS. Red clover can also regulate hormones and reduce symptoms of PMS.  It's essential to note that while these herbs can help with PMS symptoms, they should not replace medical treatment. Always consult a healthcare professional before using medicinal herbs, especially if you're pregnant or breastfeeding.
